content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

June 2, 2017


Katharine L. Elliott
County Counsel
Administration Center
501 Low Gap Road, Room 1030
Ukiah, CA 95482

Re:     Your Request for Advice
        Our File No. A-17-140

Dear Ms. Elliott:

        This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act")¹ and Section 1090. Please note that we do not advise on any other area of law, including Public Contract Code or common law conflicts of interest. We are also not a finder of fact when considering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate.

        Regarding our advice on Section 1090, we are required to forward your request and all pertinent facts relating to the request to the Attorney General's Office and the Mendocino County District Attorney's Office, which we have done. (Section 1097.1(c)(3).) We did not receive a written response from either entity. (Section 1097.1(c)(4).) We are also required to advise you that, for purposes of Section 1090, the following advice "is not admissible in a criminal proceeding against any individual other than the requestor." (See Section 1097.1(c)(5).)

QUESTIONS

1. Does the Act prohibit Supervisor Croskey from taking part in governmental decisions relating to collective bargaining agreements covering Sheriff's Office, approval of the Sheriff's Office budget, or purchase of equipment for the Sheriff's office given that the Supervisor's husband is employed at the Sheriff's Office?

2. Does Section 1090 prohibit Supervisor Croskey from making or participating in the making of, or the County from entering into, a collective bargaining agreement to be made by the County with the Deputy Sheriff's Association?

_______________
¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-17-140
Page No. 2

CONCLUSIONS

1. No. The Act does not prohibit Supervisor Croskey from taking part in decisions relating to the collective bargaining agreement so long as the decisions apply equally to all Sheriff's Office employees in the same bargaining unit or representative group as the Supervisor's husband. Also, the Act does not prohibit the Supervisor from taking part in governmental decisions relating to approval of the Sheriff's Office budget or purchase of Sheriff's Office equipment unless the decisions would have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect upon the personal finances of the Supervisor or her immediate family.

2. No. Section 1090 does not prohibit Supervisor Croskey from making or participating in the making of, or the County from entering into, the collective bargaining agreement because Section 1091.5(a)(6)'s noninterest exception applies.

FACTS

You are the Mendocino County Counsel, and you are seeking advice on behalf of Supervisor Georgeanne Croskey as the attorney for the Board of Supervisors. Supervisor Croskey was recently appointed by the Governor to serve as the Supervisor for the County's Third District. Her husband is employed by the County as a Deputy Sheriff Coroner II, and has held this position for more than one year prior to Supervisor Croskey's appointment. The County is currently engaged in labor talks with the Deputy Sheriff's Association to form a collective bargaining agreement. The County is also in the process of establishing a budget for the 2017-2018 fiscal year, which includes several items which may affect the employment of Supervisor Croskey's husband such as the potential purchase of new equipment for the Sheriff's Office, but these do not involve contracts at this time.

Supervisor Croskey is requesting advice on whether her husband's employment would prohibit her from the following:

1) Voting or participating in the approval of a collective bargaining agreement with the Deputy Sheriff's Association.
2) The approval of the Sheriff's office budget.
3) The approval of the purchase of equipment to be used by the Sheriff's office.

ANALYSIS

The Act

Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in the making, or otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial interest. Section 87103 provides that an official has a "financial interest" in a decision, within the meaning of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect on one or more of the official's interests identified in that section.

Under Section 87103, an official always has an interest in his or her personal finances and those of immediate family members. Section 82029 defines "immediate family" as the spouse and

File No. A-17-140
Page No. 3

dependent children. Therefore, the Supervisor Croskey has an interest in her personal finances and
those of her husband with respect to the decisions at issue.²

Foreseeability and Materiality

A financial effect on an official's interest is presumed to be reasonably foreseeable if the
interest is a named party in, or the subject of, a decision before the official's agency. (Regulation
18701(a).) An interest is the subject of a proceeding if the decision involves the issuance, renewal,
approval, denial, or revocation of any license, permit, or other entitlement to, or contract with, the
official's interest. (Ibid.)

Regulation 18702.5(a) provides that a "personal financial effect" means the effect of a
decision on the personal finances of an official or his or her immediate family, and that such an
effect is material if the official or the official's immediate family member will receive a measurable
financial benefit or loss from the decision. However, Regulation 18702.5(b)(1) provides that a
"personal financial effect" does not include any establishment of, or change to, benefits provided
under an employment or retirement policy for employees or retirees if the decision's effect applies
equally to all employees in the same bargaining unit or other representative group.

The County is currently engaged in labor talks with the Deputy Sheriff's Association, will
be establishing a budget for the 2017-2018 fiscal year, and will have decisions before it involving
approval of the purchase of equipment to be used by the Sheriff's office and its deputies. If the
decisions regarding the agreements would result in a measurable financial benefit or loss with
respect to the Supervisor's or her immediate family's personal finances, the personal financial
effect of those decisions would be material. However, if the decisions' effect applies equally to all
employees in the same bargaining unit or other representative group, it would not be material.
Therefore, the Act does not prohibit the Supervisor from taking part in the decisions regarding the
collective bargaining agreement so long as those decisions apply equally to all employees in the
same bargaining unit or representative group as her husband.

About the budget and the approval of the purchase of equipment for the Sheriff's office,
Supervisor Croskey would not have a conflict so long as there is no measurable financial benefit or
loss with respect to her personal finances or the personal finances of her immediate family.

If there would be a measurable benefit or loss to Supervisor Croskey or her immediate
family as a result of the budget decisions, consistent with our past advice, Supervisor Croskey may
participate in the budget approval. Regulation 18706(c) provides "Once all the separate decisions
related to a budget ... have been finalized, the public official may participate in the final vote to
adopt or reject the agency's budget...." (See Sarton Advice Letter, No. A-17-027.)

² Because Section 82030(b)(2) provides that income from a local government agency does not qualify as
income under the Act, Supervisor Croskey does not have a financial interest in her spouse's employer as a source of
income.

File No. A-17-140
Page No. 4

Section 1090

Under Section 1090, "the prohibited act is the making of a contract in which the official has a financial interest." (People v. Honig (1996) 48 Cal.App.4th 289, 333.) A contract that violates Section 1090 is void. (Thomson v. Call (1985) 38 Cal.3d 633, 646.) Section 1090 is concerned with financial interests, other than remote or noninterests, that prevent public officials from exercising absolute loyalty and undivided allegiance in furthering the best interests of their agencies. (Sigall v. Taft (1962) 58 Cal.2d 565, 569.) The analysis of a financial interest under Section 1090 is separate and distinct from that applied under the Act.

We employ a six-step analysis to determine whether an official has a disqualifying conflict of interest under Section 1090. These steps are: (1) does the matter involve a public officer or employee; (2) does the matter involve a contract; (3) is the official making or participating in making a contract; (4) does the official have a financial interest in the contract; (5) does a remote or noninterest exception apply; and, under appropriate facts, (6) does the rule of necessity apply?

Steps one through four are not at issue here. This matter involves a public officer, the Supervisor, and a contract, the labor agreements. The Supervisor would be making or participating in making of the agreements because an officer is conclusively presumed to be involved in the making of his or her agency's contracts when the officer is a member of a board or commission that has the power to execute the contract at issue. (Thomson, supra, p. 649.) And the Supervisor has a financial interest in the agreements because they will govern her husband's employment by the County. Therefore, Section 1090 prohibits the Supervisor from making or participating in the making of, and the City from entering into, the agreements, unless a remote or noninterest exception or the rule of necessity applies.

Step Five: Does either a remote or noninterest exception apply?

The Legislature has created various statutory exceptions to Section 1090's prohibition. (See Sections 1091 and 1091.5) Section 1091.5 sets forth noninterest exceptions to the prohibition. If a noninterest is present, the contract may be made without the officer's abstention. (City of Vernon v. Central Basin Mun. Water Dist. (1999) 69 Cal.App.4th 508, 514-515; 84 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 158, 159-160 (2001).)

Section 1091.5(a)(6) provides that an officer of a public agency shall not be deemed to be interested in a contract if the officer's interest in the contract is the officer's spouse's employment by the agency, and the spouse's employment has existed for at least one year prior to the officer's election or appointment.

The Supervisor's interest in the collective bargaining agreements is in her husband's employment by the County. The County has employed the Supervisor's husband for longer than a year before the Supervisor's appointment to the Board. Therefore, the Supervisor has a noninterest in the collective bargaining agreement and any agreements to purchase office equipment pursuant to Section 1091.5(a)(6).

File No. A-17-140
Page No. 5

Accordingly, Section 1090 does not prohibit the Supervisor from making or participating in the making of, or the Board of Supervisors from entering into, the collective bargaining agreements because Section 1091.5(a)(6)'s noninterest exception applies. Accordingly, our Section1090 analysis ends here.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Hyla P. Wagner
General Counsel

By: Sukhi R. Brar
Senior Counsel, Legal Division

SKB:jgl

sections.question:
l. Does the Act prohibit Supervisor Croskey from taking part in governmental decisions 
relating to collective bargaining agreements covering Sheriffs Office, approval of the 
Sheriffs Office budget, or purchase of equipment for the Sheriffs office given that the 
Supervisor's husband is employed at the Sherriffs Office? 
2. Does Section 1090 prohibit Supervisor Croskey from making or participating in the 
making of, or the County from entering into, a collective bargaining agreement to be 
made by the County with the Deputy Sheriffs Association?
sections.conclusion:
1. No. The Act does not prohibit Supervisor Croskey from taking part in decisions relating 
to the collective bargaining agreement so long as the decisions apply equally to all 
Sheriffs Office employees in the same bargaining unit or representative group as the 
Supervisor's husband. Also, the Act does not prohibit the Supervisor from taking part in 
governmental decisions relating to approval of the Sheriffs Office budget or purchase of 
Sheriffs Office equipment unless the decisions would have a reasonably foreseeable 
material financial effect upon the personal finances of the Supervisor or her immediate 
family. 
2. No. Section l 090 does not prohibit Supervisor Croskey from making or participating in 
the making of, or the County from entering into, the collective bargaining agreement 
because Section 109 l.5(a)(6)'s noninterest exception applies.

sections.analysis:
The Act 
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in the making, or 
otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the 
official has a financial interest. Section 87103 provides that an official has a "financial interest" in a 
decision, within the meaning of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have 
material financial effect on one or more of the official's interests identified in that section. 
Under Section 87103, an official always has an interest in his or her personal finances and 
those of immediate family members. Section 82029 defines "immediate family" as the spouse and  
dependent children. Therefore, the Supervisor Croskey has an interest in her personal finances and 
those of her husband with respect to the decisions at issue. 2 
Foreseeability and Materiality 
A financial effect on an official's interest is presumed to be reasonably foreseeable if the 
interest is a named party in, or the subject of, a decision before the official's agency. (Regulation 
1870l(a).) An interest is the subject of a proceeding if the decision involves the issuance, renewal, 
approval, denial, or revocation of any license, permit, or other entitlement to, or contract with, the 
official's interest. (Ibid.) 
Regulation 18702.5(a) provides that a "personal financial effect" means the effect of a 
decision on the personal finances of an official or his or her immediate family, and that such an 
effect is material if the official or the official's immediate family member will receive a measurable 
financial benefit or loss from the decision. However, Regulation 18702.S(b )( 1) provides that a 
"personal financial effect" does not include any establishment of, or change to, benefits provided 
under an employment or retirement policy for employees or retirees if the decision's effect applies 
equally to all employees in the same bargaining unit or other representative group. 
The County is currently engaged in labor talks with the Deputy Sheriff's Association, will 
be establishing a budget for the 2017-2018 fiscal year, and will have decisions before it involving 
approval of the purchase of equipment to be used by the Sheriff's office and its deputies. If the 
decisions regarding the agreements would result in a measurable financial benefit or loss with 
respect to the Supervisor's or her immediate family's personal finances, the personal financial 
effect of those decisions would be material. However, if the decisions' effect applies equally to all 
employees in the same bargaining unit or other representative group, it would not be material. 
Therefore, the Act does not prohibit the Supervisor from taking part in the decisions regarding the 
collective bargaining agreement so long as those decisions apply equally to all employees in the 
same bargaining unit or representative group as her husband. 
About the budget and the approval of the purchase of equipment for the Sheriff's office, 
Supervisor Croskey would not have a conflict so long as there is no measurable financial benefit or 
loss with respect to her personal finances or the personal finances of her immediate family. 
If there would be a measurable benefit or loss to Supervisor Croskey or her immediate 
family as a result of the budget decisions, consistent with our past advice, Supervisor Croskey may 
participate in the budget approval. Regulation 18706( c) provides "Once all the separate decisions 
related to a budget ... have been finalized, the public official may participate in the final vote to 
adopt or reject the agency's budget.. .. " (See Sarton Advice Letter, No. A-17-027.) 
2 Because Section 82030(b)(2) provides that income from a local government agency does not qualify as 
income under the Act, Supervisor Croskey does not have a financial interest in her spouse's employer as a source of 
income. 


Section 1090  
Under Section I 090, "the prohibited act is the making of a contract in which the official has 
a financial interest." (People v. Honig (1996) 48 Cal.App.4th 289, 333.) A contract that violates 
Section 1090 is void. (Thomson v. Call (1985) 38 Cal.3d 633, 646.) Section 1090 is concerned with 
financial interests, other than remote or noninterests, that prevent public officials from exercising 
absolute loyalty and undivided allegiance in furthering the best interests of their agencies. (Stigall v. 
Taft ( 1962) 58 Cal.2d 565, 569.) The analysis of a financial interest under Section 1090 is separate 
and distinct from that applied under the Act. 
We employ a six-step analysis to detennine whether an official _has a disqualifying conflict 
of interest under Section 1090. These steps are: (1) does the matter involve a public officer or 
employee; (2) does the matter involve a contract; (3) is the official making or participating in 
making a contract; (4) does the official have a financial interest in the contract; (5) does a remote or 
noninterest exception apply; and, under appropriate facts, (6) does the rule of necessity apply? 
Steps one through four are not at issue here. This matter involves a public officer, the 
Supervisor, and a contract, the labor agreements. The Supervisor would be making or participating 
in making of the agreements because an officer is conclusively presumed to be involved in the 
making of his or her agency's contracts when the officer is a member of a board or commission that 
has the power to execute the contract at issue. (Thomson, supra, p. 649.) And the Supervisor has a 
financial interest in the agreements because they will govern her husband's employment by the 
County. Therefore, Section 1090 prohibits the Supervisor from making or participating in the 
making of, and the City from entering into, the agreements, unless a remote or noninterest 
exception or the rule of necessity applies. 
Step Five: Does either a remote or noninterest exception apply? 
The Legislature has created various statutory exceptions to Section I 090's prohibition. (See 
Sections 1091 and 1091.5) Section 1091.5 sets forth noninterest exceptions to the prohibition. If a 
noninterest is present, the contract may be made without the officer's abstention. (City of Vernon v. 
Central Basin Mun. Water Dist. ( 1999) 69 Cal.App.4th 508, 514-515; 84 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 158, 
159-160 (2001).) 
Section 1091.5(a)(6) provides that an officer of a public agency shall not be deemed to be 
interested in a contract if the officer's interest in the contract is the officer's spouse's employment 
by the agency, and the spouse's employment has existed for at least one year prior to the officer's 
election or appointment. 
The Supervisor's interest in the collective bargaining agreements is in her husband's 
employment by the County. The County has employed the Supervisor's husband for longer than a 
year before the Supervisor's appointment to the Board. Therefore, the Supervisor has a noninterest 
in the collective bargaining agreement and any agreements to purchase office equipment pursuant to 
Section 109 l .5(a)(6).  
Accordingly, Section 1090 does not prohibit the Supervisor from making or participating in 
the making of, or the Board of Supervisors from entering into, the collective bargaining agreements 
because Section 1091.5(a)(6)'s noninterest exception applies. Accordingly, our Section 1090 
analysis ends here.

QUESTION: l. Does the Act prohibit Supervisor Croskey from taking part in governmental decisions 
relating to collective bargaining agreements covering Sheriffs Office, approval of the 
Sheriffs Office budget, or purchase of equipment for the Sheriffs office given that the 
Supervisor's husband is employed at the Sherriffs Office? 
2. Does Section 1090 prohibit Supervisor Croskey from making or participating in the 
making of, or the County from entering into, a collective bargaining agreement to be 
made by the County with the Deputy Sheriffs Association?

CONCLUSION: 1. No. The Act does not prohibit Supervisor Croskey from taking part in decisions relating 
to the collective bargaining agreement so long as the decisions apply equally to all 
Sheriffs Office employees in the same bargaining unit or representative group as the 
Supervisor's husband. Also, the Act does not prohibit the Supervisor from taking part in 
governmental decisions relating to approval of the Sheriffs Office budget or purchase of 
Sheriffs Office equipment unless the decisions would have a reasonably foreseeable 
material financial effect upon the personal finances of the Supervisor or her immediate 
family. 
2. No. Section l 090 does not prohibit Supervisor Croskey from making or participating in 
the making of, or the County from entering into, the collective bargaining agreement 
because Section 109 l.5(a)(6)'s noninterest exception applies.
